title: Port super_lazygit_rust into a workspace-first Lazygit-grade Rust TUI
description: Create the canonical execution graph for porting the planned product into `super_lazygit_rust`: a workspace-first Rust Git TUI whose repo interior mirrors Lazygit closely while workspace mode borrows the strongest multi-repo ideas from git-dash and git-scope. This root epic exists so every future bead can anchor to one explicit product contract: do not build a generic Git TUI, do not regress into a loose clone, and do not let workspace mode or repo mode drift away from the plan. The graph under this epic must preserve architectural rigor, Git correctness, testing discipline, and performance constraints for a large and precise port.
design: This root epic is the portfolio-level contract for the entire port. It exists to keep three product truths locked together throughout execution: repo mode should feel very close to Lazygit inside a single repository, workspace mode should solve the user's multi-repo pain by combining the strongest structural ideas from git-dash and git-scope, and the whole application should stay within a Rust architecture that can support realtime invalidation, testing, and future extensions without turning into a monolith. Every child epic should preserve these truths instead of optimizing a slice in isolation.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the open graph under this root can carry the implementation from empty scaffold to a credible `super_lazygit_rust` release without needing to reopen plan-space for major missing features, missing tests, or missing architectural seams. The finished graph must preserve Lazygit-grade repo UX, workspace-first multi-repo workflows, realtime freshness behavior, and a disciplined verification story.
notes: Treat this as a precision port, not a vague inspiration project. If a later implementation choice would trade away Lazygit interaction quality, remove multi-repo usefulness, or weaken Git safety without an explicit product reason, that is a graph-level regression and should be corrected before coding proceeds far.

title: Foundation: Rust workspace, app shell, reducer model, and test harnesses
description: Build the foundational Rust workspace and application skeleton for `super_lazygit_rust`. This epic covers the non-negotiable structural substrate: workspace crate layout, bootstrap path, reducer/event model shell, configuration skeleton, diagnostics hooks, and fixture harnesses. Without this, later work on workspace mode and Lazygit-style repo mode will either sprawl into a god-struct or entangle rendering with Git semantics.
design: Separate concerns early: bootstrap and reducer contracts belong in the app/core lane, rendering primitives belong in the tui lane, Git semantics stay behind the git lane, workspace orchestration stays outside rendering, and fixture support must be strong enough that later parity work can prove behavior instead of hand-waving it. This epic is intentionally front-loaded because every later area depends on having crisp boundaries.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the workspace crates, reducer contracts, app shell skeleton, theme and keybinding substrate, diagnostics hooks, and fixture helpers exist with enough clarity that later feature work can land without architectural churn. The foundation should already support unit tests, smoke tests, and controlled fixture-driven integration tests.
notes: The failure mode here is hidden coupling: if UI code can freely reach Git backends, if state is ad hoc instead of reducer-driven, or if tests require bespoke shell setup, the later port will slow down and drift. Keep this epic strict even if it feels less visible than feature work.

title: Scaffold Cargo workspace and planned crate boundaries
description: Create the actual Cargo workspace skeleton for `super_lazygit_rust` with the crate boundaries defined in the plan: app, core, tui, git, workspace, config, and test-support. This task is the first concrete implementation step because every later bead assumes those crates exist and have clear responsibility lines.
design: Create the workspace root manifest and each crate with minimal compileable shells. Keep cross-crate dependencies intentionally narrow from the start so later features do not collapse into a god-crate. Ensure there is no repo-root `main.rs` and that crate names reflect the architectural lanes from the plan.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the workspace builds, every planned crate exists, and a new contributor can identify where bootstrap, state, rendering, Git access, workspace orchestration, config, and fixtures belong.
notes: Include basic compilation smoke tests and CI-friendly commands. Failure mode to avoid: creating placeholder crates with circular dependencies that later lock the architecture into an unhealthy shape.

title: Define core app state, action enums, effects, and reducer contracts
description: Translate the plan's state model into real Rust types. This task defines AppState, WorkspaceState, RepoModeState, action/event enums, effect types, and reducer boundaries so the app can evolve through deterministic state transitions instead of ad hoc widget mutations.
design: Keep business state in . Split domain state from ephemeral view state. The reducer should be able to consume input, worker, watcher, and timer events and emit state plus effects without directly performing I/O.
acceptance_criteria: Done when reducer-driven mode transitions and placeholder state updates compile cleanly and can be unit-tested without the TUI layer.
notes: Add focused unit tests for representative transitions such as enter-repo, leave-repo, selection changes, and overlay open/close. Failure mode to avoid: allowing widgets to own authoritative Git state.

title: Build the initial app shell, mode router, and pane focus model
description: Create the terminal shell that can host workspace mode, repo mode, and modal overlays. This is the UI frame that later features plug into, so it must establish stable focus semantics, mode routing, resize behavior, and a bottom-line status/help region from the start.
design: Use ratatui and crossterm in a way that keeps rendering separate from business state. Define pane IDs, focus transitions, and modal routing explicitly rather than burying them in event handlers.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the app can boot into a stable shell, switch between placeholder modes, preserve focus identity, and render a consistent bottom status/help line.
notes: Add smoke tests or snapshot tests for initial layout states if feasible. Failure mode to avoid: mode routing logic hidden in the rendering layer.

title: Establish config, theme, and keybinding skeletons
description: Create the configuration model for workspace roots, ignores, editor command, theme defaults, and future keybinding overrides. This task exists early so later beads do not hardcode values that should live in config.
design: Implement a minimal config crate with strong defaults, a clear file location, and a schema that can grow without breaking the user. Theme and keybinding support can start skeletal, but action IDs should already be explicit so later customization does not require rewiring the app.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the app can load defaults plus an optional config file and expose typed settings to other crates without global mutable hacks.
notes: Add unit tests for default loading and malformed-config handling. Failure mode to avoid: premature over-configuration that makes the product harder to reason about.

title: Add diagnostics, logging, and performance timing hooks
description: Install the observability hooks needed to understand startup time, scan cost, Git operation latency, watcher churn, and rendering hotspots. This work must happen early because later performance and failure analysis depend on it.
design: Expose structured logging and optional debug timing around scan, refresh, diff load, and long Git operations. Keep the instrumentation cheap in normal mode and richer in debug mode.
acceptance_criteria: Done when there is a consistent way to inspect startup timing, worker latency, and watcher health without editing the code ad hoc during every investigation.
notes: Include at least one diagnostic smoke path and ensure logs do not spam by default. Failure mode to avoid: invisible performance regressions that only surface after many features land.

title: Create temp-repo fixture and topology helpers in test-support
description: Build the `test-support` helpers that can rapidly create clean repos, dirty repos, staged and unstaged mixes, upstream divergence, conflicts, stashes, and worktrees. Nearly every later bead depends on synthetic Git topologies to test correctness without depending on manual shell setup.
design: Provide high-level helpers for repo init, file writes, commits, branch creation, upstream simulation, and conflict generation. Keep these helpers ergonomic enough that future tests use them by default instead of copy-pasting raw Git commands.
acceptance_criteria: Done when downstream beads can create representative Git scenarios through reusable helpers rather than bespoke setup in each test.
notes: Cover at least clean, dirty, staged, and ahead/behind fixtures immediately. Failure mode to avoid: a weak fixture layer that makes precise Git tests too painful to write.

title: Git engine parity: facade, backend routing, and safe Git semantics
description: Create the Git engine and parity layer that will let `super_lazygit_rust` behave like real Git while still staying fast enough for multi-repo workspace use. This epic covers the `GitFacade`, backend routing between `gix`, `git2`, and Git CLI, and the explicit parity decisions needed for status, diff, commit, fetch, pull, push, and patch-oriented operations.
design: Use this epic to create a stable Git seam that can support both fast workspace summaries and richer repo-detail interactions without leaking backend-specific types into the UI. Reads, writes, parity quirks, and fallback rules should all become explicit contracts. The engine should serve the Lazygit-like UX rather than forcing the UI to understand low-level Git library details.
acceptance_criteria: Done when callers can obtain summary data, detailed repo views, and write-operation results through stable facade contracts, when backend mismatch behavior is documented and testable, and when the selected Git backend strategy is strong enough to support later staging, sync, history, and rebase flows without redesign.
notes: This epic is one of the main architectural chokepoints in the graph. If the facade is underspecified, later repo mode and history mode beads will compensate with UI-specific hacks. Keep failure surfaces, progress reporting, and backend fallback semantics explicit.

title: Define the GitFacade traits and backend-routing contract
description: Create the facade API that isolates all callers from direct `git2`, `gix`, or Git CLI access. This task sets the permanent seam for status reads, detail reads, diffs, and write operations and therefore determines whether later work remains coherent.
design: Model separate read and write responsibilities, normalize result types, and encode operation progress/error shapes that the UI can consume. Be explicit about which calls are summary-only, detail-only, or write-capable.
acceptance_criteria: Done when other crates can depend on facade traits and typed result models without importing backend-specific types.
notes: Add interface-level tests or compile checks that enforce the no-backend-leak rule. Failure mode to avoid: UI code reaching around the facade because the contract is incomplete.

title: Implement workspace summary readers for branch, dirty, ahead/behind, and fetch age
description: Implement the high-volume read path used by workspace mode. This task should produce lightweight RepoSummary data including branch, dirty counts, ahead/behind, remote shorthand, and fetch-age information so the dashboard can become useful quickly.
design: Prefer fast local reads and summary-oriented backends. Normalize no-upstream, detached HEAD, inaccessible repo, and never-fetched cases so the UI does not need special ad hoc logic.
acceptance_criteria: Done when workspace mode can obtain trustworthy repo summaries quickly across many repositories and represent edge cases explicitly instead of flattening them into false-clean states.
notes: Back this with fixture-driven tests for clean, dirty, detached, ahead, behind, diverged, and missing-upstream repos. Failure mode to avoid: silently misreporting sync state.

title: Implement repo detail readers for status trees, diffs, branches, commits, stash, and reflog
description: Implement the heavier read APIs needed by repo mode and history/reference views. This includes working tree status, staged status, diff inputs, branch lists, commit lists, stash lists, reflog items, and worktree metadata where practical.
design: Use one normalized set of domain models so status, diff, history, and branch views can compose instead of each building their own bespoke parser stack. Be explicit about pagination or lazy-load boundaries where large repos may matter.
acceptance_criteria: Done when repo mode can obtain structured detail data without shelling out from the UI layer and without each view inventing its own data source contract.
notes: Cover representative fixtures including staged/unstaged mixes, stash presence, branch lists, and reflog data. Failure mode to avoid: detail models that are view-specific and impossible to reuse.

title: Implement core write operations for commit, checkout, fetch, pull, and push
description: Implement the first set of write operations that the product needs for daily usefulness: commit, amend head if straightforward, branch checkout, fetch, pull, and push. These operations must surface progress and errors cleanly and must preserve Git semantics that users already trust.
design: Route through the facade, not directly from views. Prefer Git CLI where hooks, credential helpers, or transport semantics matter. Ensure write results trigger the right follow-up refresh behavior.
acceptance_criteria: Done when core write flows can execute from facade tests with explicit success/failure results and enough metadata for UI confirmations and toasts.
notes: Add integration tests for missing upstream, auth-free local remotes where possible, branch checkout, and commit flows. Failure mode to avoid: fire-and-forget writes that leave the UI in stale state.

title: Run a patch and hunk-application parity spike for selective staging
description: Investigate and implement the backend path for hunk-level staging and unstaging. This is a parity spike because selective staging is central to the port, but backend semantics can drift badly between libraries and Git CLI.
design: Prototype the chosen path behind the facade, document edge cases like reverse patching and untracked files, and determine whether , CLI patch application, or a hybrid route is safest.
acceptance_criteria: Done when there is a clearly chosen selective-staging path with fixture-backed evidence and explicit notes about unsupported or deferred edge cases.
notes: Include tests for multi-hunk files, reverse apply behavior, and failure cases. Failure mode to avoid: claiming hunk parity without reliable patch semantics.

title: Codify backend mismatch rules, fallback paths, and parity tests
description: Capture the places where , , and Git CLI do not behave identically and turn them into executable safeguards. This task exists so the port remains precise over time rather than relying on remembered tribal knowledge.
design: Write explicit tests and decision notes for mismatch cases. Where behavior is uncertain, force the facade to choose a conservative fallback instead of letting callers guess.
acceptance_criteria: Done when risky mismatch areas have named tests or documented fallback rules and future contributors can see why one backend was chosen over another.
notes: Failure mode to avoid: silently regressing from Git-correct behavior after a later refactor.

title: Workspace mode: discovery, dashboard, search, filters, and triage UX
description: Implement workspace mode as the product's command center. This epic covers discovery, cached startup, repo registry, worker-based refresh, table rendering, search/filter/sort, preview panes, and workspace switching. The goal is to solve the user's multi-repo blindness before repo-mode depth work even begins.
design: Workspace mode should not be a decorative repo picker. It should give the user a durable command center for many repositories: discovery, caching, refresh orchestration, searchable and sortable repo tables, preview context, and graceful handling of incomplete scans. The information density and keyboard flow should feel intentional and consistent with the Lazygit visual language even though the layout is more dashboard-like.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the app can discover repositories from configured roots, preserve stable identities, render a useful workspace table, support fast triage through search/filter/sort, preview the selected repo, and survive loading or partial-failure states without confusing the operator. Transitioning from workspace mode into repo mode should feel immediate and predictable.
notes: The core product differentiation lives here. Avoid building a generic list plus metadata dump. The user needs a mode that meaningfully reduces multi-repo context switching, surfaces attention-worthy repositories quickly, and still feels visually and behaviorally consistent with the repo interior.

title: Implement recursive repo discovery with `.git` dir and gitdir-file support
description: Build workspace discovery so the app can scan configured roots, identify Git repositories, respect gitdir files for worktrees/submodules, and stop descending once a repo root has been found. This is the entry point for workspace usefulness.
design: Support canonical path resolution, display-path retention, ignore globs, and nested-repo stop rules. Treat inaccessible directories and broken gitdir files as partial failures instead of whole-scan aborts.
acceptance_criteria: Done when configured roots produce a stable set of repo identities suitable for caching and summary refresh across normal and worktree-style repositories.
notes: Add integration tests for nested repos, gitdir-file repos, and symlink-friendly paths where practical. Failure mode to avoid: scanning into nested repos repeatedly or missing worktree-style repos.

title: Build the workspace cache and stable repo registry
description: Implement the cache-backed startup path and registry that remember previously seen repos, their last known summaries, and the workspace-root fingerprint. This task is what makes workspace mode feel immediate instead of blocked on a cold scan every launch.
design: Store enough metadata to render stale-but-useful summaries quickly, then refresh in background. Keep cache invalidation simple and explicit. The repo registry should provide stable IDs independent of row ordering.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the app can warm-start from cache, detect workspace-root changes, and expose stable repo IDs and summaries to the rest of the system.
notes: Add tests for cache hit, cache miss, stale cache, and root mismatch cases. Failure mode to avoid: cache corruption or path instability causing duplicate repo identities.

title: Implement background summary refresh workers and scheduling
description: Create the worker-pool orchestration that refreshes repo summaries off the UI thread. This task should mirror the pragmatic strength of git-dash while fitting the reducer/effect architecture from the foundation epic.
design: Use bounded worker concurrency, explicit job types, and event-driven completion notifications back into core state. Do not let long-running repos freeze the list or block unrelated refreshes.
acceptance_criteria: Done when summary refresh can run across many repos concurrently with progress/error signals returned to state management cleanly.
notes: Add tests or harness checks for partial failures and worker completion ordering. Failure mode to avoid: hidden thread coordination logic that the reducer cannot reason about.

title: Render the workspace repo table and selection model
description: Implement the dense repo table that becomes the default home screen. This task covers row rendering, selection movement, width-sensitive column presentation, focus behavior, and the basic table-driven visual language for the multi-repo command center.
design: Prioritize repo name, branch, dirty summary, ahead/behind, and fetch age. Keep the table scan-friendly and avoid decorative noise. Preserve enough styling continuity with Lazygit that repo-mode transition later feels natural.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the user can move through a live repo table, understand row state at a glance, and preserve selection across refreshes.
notes: Add snapshot coverage for wide and narrow layouts if practical. Failure mode to avoid: a pretty table that hides the most important operational signals.

title: Add workspace search, filter, sort, and attention-score behavior
description: Implement the triage behaviors that make workspace mode a real command center: fuzzy-ish search, dirty/ahead/behind/conflict filters, sort modes, and a default attention score that surfaces repos needing action first.
design: Keep the rules explicit enough that users can understand why a repo floated upward. Search and filters must not fight each other or destroy orientation.
acceptance_criteria: Done when a user can quickly narrow many repos down to the ones that matter and the default ordering feels operationally smart rather than arbitrary.
notes: Add reducer tests for search/filter/sort combinations and edge cases like no-upstream or error rows. Failure mode to avoid: opaque ranking that undermines trust.

title: Implement selected-repo preview pane and workspace-to-repo transition
description: Build the preview experience for the selected repo and the enter/return flow between workspace mode and repo mode. This task is about orientation: users should see summary context before diving in and should return to the same workspace context after deep work.
design: Support at least one preview mode immediately, with room for recent commits or file-change previews later. Preserve selected row, scroll position, filters, and search when returning from repo mode.
acceptance_criteria: Done when entering repo mode feels like zooming in and returning feels lossless in terms of workspace context.
notes: Include reducer tests for enter/leave transitions and selection restoration. Failure mode to avoid: context loss when hopping between repos.

title: Handle workspace loading, empty, partial-failure, and switching flows
description: Finish the operational states around workspace mode: loading indicators, no-repo states, partial failures, and workspace-root switching. The app must remain comprehensible when scan results are incomplete or empty.
design: Expose scan progress, current root, and clear next actions in empty states. For partial failures, keep successful rows visible and let users inspect broken ones. Workspace switching should be explicit and recoverable.
acceptance_criteria: Done when workspace mode communicates what is happening during scans and does not collapse into ambiguity when repos are missing or broken.
notes: Add snapshot or interaction tests for empty, loading, and partial-failure states. Failure mode to avoid: a blank screen or silent degradation during scan problems.

title: Realtime freshness: watchers, invalidation, debounce, and fallback polling
description: Add realtime freshness to workspace and repo state through watcher-driven invalidation, debounced refresh scheduling, visibility-aware priorities, and fallback polling. This epic exists to solve stale-state trust problems without introducing watcher churn, UI freezes, or high-resource background behavior.
design: Realtime freshness should combine push-style invalidation where possible with pragmatic scheduling and fallback polling where watches degrade. The architecture must recognize that not every filesystem or repository topology is equally watchable, so backend health, debounce policy, visibility-aware refresh, and degraded-mode UX all need first-class treatment instead of being bolted on afterward.
acceptance_criteria: Done when repository state can refresh promptly after real changes, when noisy change storms are debounced into sane refresh work, when active and visible repos receive stronger freshness guarantees than hidden ones, and when degraded watcher environments fall back to polling with clear operator feedback instead of silent staleness.
notes: This epic exists to solve the user's realtime pain without destabilizing the app. The main failure modes are stale state, thrashing refresh loops, and watch behavior that looks magical in demos but breaks in large workspaces. Favor explicit health reporting and measurable freshness semantics.

title: Implement watcher abstraction and backend health reporting
description: Create the watcher abstraction that hides backend details and emits normalized invalidation signals plus health state. This task is the gateway to realtime freshness and must avoid leaking platform-specific watcher quirks into core logic or UI components.
design: Watchers should produce repo-scoped invalidation events and explicit degraded or recovered health signals. Keep the abstraction testable with fake event sources so later debounce and fallback logic can be validated without real filesystem churn.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the system can register watches, emit normalized invalidation events, and report backend health without UI code touching watcher internals.
notes: Add tests around synthetic watcher events and health transitions. Failure mode to avoid: platform-specific watcher assumptions hardcoded into state logic.

title: Build per-repo invalidation debounce and refresh scheduling
description: Implement the debounce layer that converts noisy file events into bounded repo refresh requests. This task is where watcher realism matters: editors and Git internals can generate bursts, so the scheduler must coalesce rather than thrash.
design: Group events by repo ID, debounce aggressively enough to avoid storms, and route refresh jobs back into the same effect pipeline as manual or periodic refreshes. Keep the logic deterministic enough for reducer and scheduler tests.
acceptance_criteria: Done when repeated file events for one repo become a sensible refresh cadence instead of a refresh flood.
notes: Add tests for bursty event sequences and coalescing behavior. Failure mode to avoid: every save event causing multiple redundant refreshes.

title: Add visibility-aware refresh priority for active, visible, and hidden repos
description: Teach the refresh system to prioritize the repo the user is actively viewing, then visible workspace rows, then hidden repos. This task ensures realtime freshness improves trust without wasting resources on repos the user cannot currently see.
design: Use the current mode, selected repo, and visible table window as explicit priority inputs. Integrate with worker scheduling rather than creating a side scheduler that fights the main refresh path.
acceptance_criteria: Done when active repos refresh first, visible repos refresh promptly, and hidden repos can lag without degrading the UX.
notes: Add state and scheduler tests that prove priority ordering. Failure mode to avoid: hidden repos consuming worker capacity while the focused repo waits.

title: Implement fallback polling and freshness badges for degraded watch mode
description: Provide a safe fallback when the watcher backend fails or is unavailable. Users must still trust the dashboard, so the app needs timer-based refresh plus explicit freshness or degraded badges instead of silently going stale.
design: Expose watch health and freshness state in both workspace mode and repo mode. Poll conservatively to preserve low-resource behavior. Make the degraded state obvious but not noisy.
acceptance_criteria: Done when a watcher failure no longer leaves the app silently stale and the user can see whether state is live or on fallback polling.
notes: Add tests for degraded-to-recovered transitions. Failure mode to avoid: stale state presented as live state.

title: Repo mode core parity: status, diff, staging, commit, and sync loop
description: Implement the core repo-mode experience so it feels recognizably close to Lazygit for the main daily loop: inspect working tree, inspect staged set, inspect diff, stage/unstage precisely, commit, and sync. This epic is where the port must become unmistakably useful to Lazygit users.
design: Repo mode should inherit the strongest Lazygit interaction patterns as directly as the new architecture allows. The pane layout, focus traversal, diff reading flow, staging actions, commit box behavior, and sync confirmations should all feel familiar to experienced Lazygit users while remaining internally reducer-driven and testable. This is the core UX parity epic.
acceptance_criteria: Done when a user can enter repo mode and perform the common inner loop of triaging file changes, inspecting diffs, staging and unstaging work, composing commits, and running fetch/pull/push actions through a layout and focus system that feels close to Lazygit rather than merely inspired by it.
notes: Do not let visual similarity substitute for behavioral parity. If pane focus, staging semantics, diff navigation, or sync confirmations feel inconsistent with Lazygit expectations, the port is still incomplete even if screenshots look close.

title: Build repo mode shell and Lazygit-like focus model
description: Create the structural shell for repo mode so the user can move between working tree, staged tree, diff, and overlays in a way that feels close to Lazygit. This task establishes the mental model for all deeper repo interactions.
design: Mirror Lazygit panel relationships closely while fitting the new reducer-based architecture. Keep focus transitions explicit and ensure the bottom line reflects current pane actions and disabled reasons.
acceptance_criteria: Done when repo mode can render a stable status screen shell with predictable pane focus and overlay routing.
notes: Add snapshot or smoke coverage for focus transitions. Failure mode to avoid: repo mode that looks like Lazygit but behaves inconsistently.

title: Implement working tree and staged tree panes
description: Render the two core tree panes for repo mode: working tree and staged content. These panes are the left-hand backbone of the Lazygit-style daily workflow and must make file state obvious before the user ever reads the diff pane.
design: Support selection, directory collapse and expand, file state markers, and synchronization with the shared repo detail models. Preserve selection sensibly across refreshes.
acceptance_criteria: Done when both panes render actionable file trees that stay aligned with repo status updates and can drive downstream stage, unstage, and diff actions.
notes: Add tests or snapshots for modified, added, deleted, untracked, and conflicted rows. Failure mode to avoid: tree state drifting from the underlying repo detail model.

title: Implement repo diff viewer with scroll, focus, and hunk boundaries
description: Build the diff pane that presents the selected file or hunk with enough clarity to support precise staging. This is one of the highest-value pieces of repo mode because it replaces the need to open an IDE just to understand changes.
design: Support scrolling, focus changes, hunk boundary display, and future hooks for line selection. Keep rendering efficient enough for larger diffs and preserve a path toward configurable context size later.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the selected file or status item can drive a readable diff pane that the user can scroll and return from without losing orientation.
notes: Add snapshot coverage for representative diffs. Failure mode to avoid: diff rendering that is too slow or too visually ambiguous for precise staging.

title: Add file-level stage and unstage actions
description: Implement the simplest actionable staging loop: file-level stage and unstage from the repo-mode trees. This provides immediate usefulness before hunk and line precision are complete.
design: Route through the GitFacade, refresh the affected repo detail state after writes, and make state transitions visible in both working tree and staged panes.
acceptance_criteria: Done when users can stage and unstage whole files reliably from repo mode and immediately see the result reflected in both panes.
notes: Add integration tests for modified, added, and untracked files. Failure mode to avoid: file-level actions that do not synchronize the UI correctly after the write.

title: Add hunk-level stage and unstage actions
description: Implement selective staging at the hunk level using the backend strategy chosen in the patch parity spike. This is a critical parity step because messy commits are one of the main pain points driving the project.
design: Integrate hunk selection from the diff pane and ensure stage and unstage paths both behave correctly. Be explicit about unsupported edge cases until they are truly handled.
acceptance_criteria: Done when users can move individual hunks between working tree and staged state with repeatable semantics and visible UI feedback.
notes: Add integration tests for multi-hunk files, reverse operations, and failure cases. Failure mode to avoid: partial hunk operations that silently corrupt stage state.

title: Implement commit box, commit validation, and amend-head flow
description: Build the inline commit flow so users can write a commit message and commit without leaving repo mode. This task should also support amend-head where semantics are straightforward and safe.
design: Keep commit entry inside repo mode and make validation clear. Commit results must refresh status immediately and preserve user orientation rather than dropping them into an ambiguous post-commit state.
acceptance_criteria: Done when staged changes can be committed from repo mode and amend-head is available with explicit validation and refresh behavior.
notes: Add integration tests for normal commit and amend-head. Failure mode to avoid: commit success with stale panes still visible afterward.

title: Wire repo-mode fetch, pull, and push flows with confirmations and refresh
description: Bring transport actions into repo mode so the user can fetch, pull, and push from the same context where they inspect diffs and commits. This closes the fragmented commit and sync loop called out in the pain points.
design: Use confirmation overlays where needed, preserve transport progress and errors, and trigger the appropriate post-operation refresh logic. Missing upstream and transport failures must be first-class states.
acceptance_criteria: Done when repo mode can fetch, pull, and push with explicit feedback and trustworthy follow-up state.
notes: Add tests or harnesses for missing upstream and local transport success paths. Failure mode to avoid: sync actions that succeed or fail without enough context for the user to recover.

title: History and reference views: branches, commits, stash, reflog, worktrees
description: Add the history and reference views that make the terminal sufficient for everyday repo archaeology and branch management: branches, commits, stash, reflog, worktrees, and comparison flows. This epic converts repo mode from a commit box into a serious Git workspace.
design: These views should extend the Lazygit-like repo interior rather than feeling like detached tools. Branches, commits, stash, reflog, worktrees, and compare flows should reuse shared selection, preview, diff, and action infrastructure wherever possible so the user experiences one coherent application instead of separate mini-modes.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the operator can browse and act on branches, commit history, stash entries, reflog movement, worktrees, and compare targets through stable keyboard flows, shared visual patterns, and Git-correct action handling that is compatible with later power-user operations.
notes: This epic is where many Git tools become inconsistent. Resist one-off views with bespoke behavior. Reuse infrastructure aggressively so history and reference work feels native to the rest of repo mode and remains cheap to test.

title: Implement branches view with checkout, create, rename, delete, and upstream actions
description: Create the branch-management view that lets users inspect local branch state and perform the core branch actions without dropping to shell. This is a major parity requirement for a serious Git TUI.
design: Render branch rows clearly, surface current branch and upstream information, and route write actions through the same confirmation and refresh pipeline used elsewhere in repo mode.
acceptance_criteria: Done when normal branch management flows are available and the view stays coherent after branch-changing operations.
notes: Add integration coverage for checkout, create, rename, delete, and upstream edge cases. Failure mode to avoid: branch actions that mutate Git state but leave the view inconsistent.

title: Implement commits view with files preview and compare-ready state
description: Create the commit-history view for everyday archaeology: browse commits, inspect file lists, and establish the selection state needed for compare or cherry-pick later. This closes the gap between a plain log and a real TUI history workflow.
design: Use normalized commit models from the facade and keep the view composable with the shared diff infrastructure. Do not prematurely overbuild commit graph rendering if it destabilizes the baseline view.
acceptance_criteria: Done when users can browse commits, open a selected commit's changed files, and inspect those changes without leaving the app.
notes: Add fixture-backed tests for commit list ordering and commit-file inspection. Failure mode to avoid: a commit view that cannot scale or cannot hand off cleanly to compare and power-user flows.

title: Implement stash view and stash apply and drop flows
description: Build the stash view so users can see stashes, inspect them, and perform normal apply or drop actions. Stash behavior is a place where backend semantics can differ, so the UI contract must stay honest about what the backend actually guarantees.
design: Present stash entries clearly and route apply or drop through explicit progress and error reporting. Tie into the shared diff or detail infrastructure rather than building a stash-only rendering path.
acceptance_criteria: Done when stash entries are visible and normal stash lifecycle actions are usable from repo mode.
notes: Add integration tests around stash presence and apply or drop behavior where stable. Failure mode to avoid: UI promises that exceed the backend parity guarantees.

title: Implement reflog view for recovery and recent movement inspection
description: Create the reflog view so users can inspect recent HEAD or ref movements and regain orientation after checkout, reset, rebase, or recovery operations. This supports trust and recovery even before full undo features land.
design: Present reflog items in a compact, readable format and allow diff or detail navigation where supported by the shared infrastructure.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the user can browse recent reflog history from within repo mode and use it to understand recent repo movement.
notes: Add integration coverage for non-empty reflog scenarios. Failure mode to avoid: reflog information being so thin that it provides no recovery value.

title: Implement worktrees view and basic create or remove flows
description: Add worktree visibility and the first level of worktree actions to repo mode. Worktrees matter for multi-branch workflows and are a useful differentiator for heavy terminal users.
design: List worktree path and branch relationships clearly. Keep destructive removal behind explicit confirmation. If create support is too large for one pass, ensure the view at least makes existing worktrees visible and understandable.
acceptance_criteria: Done when users can inspect worktrees and at least perform the basic safe lifecycle operations planned for this phase.
notes: Add tests around worktree discovery and safe removal preconditions where practical. Failure mode to avoid: destructive worktree actions with weak visibility into what will be removed.

title: Implement compare refs and commits mode using shared diff infrastructure
description: Build the compare workflow that lets the user mark one ref or commit and inspect the diff against another. This is an important parity feature because it makes commit history exploration much more actionable than a plain list.
design: Reuse the existing diff infrastructure and keep compare state explicit in core state. The user should always understand what is being compared and how to exit compare mode.
acceptance_criteria: Done when a user can select a comparison target, inspect the resulting diff, and return to normal history browsing cleanly.
notes: Add reducer and integration tests for entering and leaving compare mode. Failure mode to avoid: sticky compare state that contaminates normal history navigation.

title: Power-user parity: line staging, rebase, cherry-pick, amend, reset, undo
description: Plan and implement the advanced power-user features required for credible parity with Lazygit: line staging, cherry-pick, revert, interactive rebase, older-commit amend/fixup flows, dangerous reset/discard menus, and eventual undo/redo where semantics are defensible. This is where the port graduates from “useful” to “serious”.
design: Treat this as the careful extension layer for advanced Git workflows that users expect from serious terminals, not as a dumping ground for risky actions. Line staging, cherry-pick or revert, interactive rebase, amend workflows, reset or discard variants, and undo boundaries must all ride on strong Git semantics, explicit warnings, and reusable overlay mechanics.
acceptance_criteria: Done when advanced workflows can be executed through safe, comprehensible UI flows that preserve operator trust, clearly communicate destructive risk, and integrate with the same repo-mode mental model established earlier in the graph. Power-user capability should increase depth, not increase confusion.
notes: The temptation here is to add surface area faster than safety guarantees. Do not. If an advanced operation cannot yet be explained, previewed, tested, and recovered sensibly, the bead should stay blocked until the supporting infrastructure is stronger.

title: Implement line-level staging on top of stable hunk semantics
description: Add line-level staging once file and hunk staging are already trustworthy. This is one of the signature Lazygit features and needs to land with precision, not approximation.
design: Use the hunk-staging foundation and explicit selection state in the diff pane. Be honest about any hard edge cases and avoid claiming arbitrary line slicing if the backend cannot support it safely yet.
acceptance_criteria: Done when users can stage or unstage selected lines in common cases with repeatable behavior and clear UI feedback.
notes: Add integration coverage for multi-line and boundary cases. Failure mode to avoid: line operations that stage more than the user selected.

title: Implement cherry-pick and revert flows from commit history
description: Add cherry-pick and revert operations to the commits view so users can perform common history-transfer and rollback operations without leaving the TUI. These actions need careful error and conflict handling because they can leave the repo in transient states.
design: Route through the GitFacade with explicit progress and follow-up refresh behavior. Make it obvious when the repo enters a conflicted or in-progress state after one of these actions.
acceptance_criteria: Done when normal cherry-pick and revert operations can be initiated from the commits view and their results are surfaced clearly.
notes: Add integration tests for straightforward cherry-pick and revert paths. Failure mode to avoid: starting an operation without surfacing the resulting transient state.

title: Implement interactive rebase mode with continue, abort, and skip pathways
description: Build an explicit interactive rebase flow rather than scattering rebase logic across commit actions. This is a high-value parity feature and one of the easiest places to ship something dangerous if the state model is weak.
design: Treat rebase as a mode with visible progress, continue, abort, and skip actions. Make in-progress rebase state impossible to ignore. Route through the parity-aware backend layer rather than a UI-local shell hack.
acceptance_criteria: Done when an interactive rebase can be started, inspected, continued, aborted, or skipped with the current state visible to the user.
notes: Add fixture-backed tests for entering and resuming a rebase state. Failure mode to avoid: hidden rebase state with no clear recovery path.

title: Implement older-commit amend, fixup, and reword flows
description: Add the higher-order history cleanup flows that depend on rebase support: amend an older commit, fixup or squash support, and reword-like edits. These are part of what makes Lazygit compelling to advanced users.
design: Build these flows on top of the explicit rebase mode and reuse commit-selection semantics from the history view. Safety and state visibility are more important than covering every edge case in the first pass.
acceptance_criteria: Done when older-commit amend and related fixup style flows exist as explicit actions built on a visible rebase-oriented state machine.
notes: Add focused tests for basic amend or fixup pathways. Failure mode to avoid: retroactive history edits with poor visibility into what commit is actually being changed.

title: Implement reset, discard, and nuke flows with strong safety overlays
description: Add the dangerous cleanup actions users eventually need: discard file changes, reset variants, and full working-tree cleanup. These are powerful recovery tools but must never feel casual or ambiguous.
design: Group destructive actions behind explicit overlays that explain scope, reversibility, and target. Reuse shared confirmation patterns rather than inventing special one-off prompts.
acceptance_criteria: Done when dangerous actions are available, clearly scoped, and protected by strong confirmations and follow-up refresh behavior.
notes: Add tests for confirmation gating and representative destructive success cases where safe test fixtures allow it. Failure mode to avoid: one-key destructive actions without context.

title: Investigate and implement safe undo or redo boundaries
description: Decide what undo and redo can mean honestly in this product and implement only the defensible subset. This task exists because users value a safety net, but a misleading undo story would damage trust more than having no undo at all.
design: Use reflog-aware semantics where valid and explicitly exclude flows that cannot be reversed safely. Document limits in both code and user-facing help.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the product exposes only those undo or redo actions that map cleanly to real Git recovery semantics and clearly states the limits.
notes: Add tests around supported undo boundaries and explicit documentation for unsupported cases. Failure mode to avoid: claiming undo for working tree changes that are not actually recoverable.

title: Testing, performance, hardening, and operator-facing documentation
description: Ensure the implementation is trustworthy through explicit reducer tests, Git fixture integration coverage, watcher tests, TUI end-to-end flows, performance profiling, failure-mode coverage, and documentation. This epic exists because a Git TUI without disciplined tests and diagnostics will eventually betray user trust.
design: This epic is the trust layer for the whole port. It should prove reducer correctness, Git topology correctness, watcher correctness, keyboard-flow correctness, performance behavior, failure handling, and operator usability through targeted harnesses rather than broad promises. Documentation also belongs here because the product cannot be considered complete if users and future contributors cannot operate it confidently.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the graph includes credible unit, integration, end-to-end, performance, and failure-mode coverage for the most important workflows, and when README, keybinding, config, and troubleshooting documentation are strong enough that an operator can understand both normal usage and degraded scenarios.
notes: Testing and docs are not cleanup work to postpone until the end. Several downstream epics depend on this one because proof of correctness, logging quality, and operator guidance are part of product scope for a Git tool that aims to replace muscle-memory workflows.

title: Add reducer and state-model unit tests
description: Create the baseline unit-test suite for reducers, action handling, selection movement, overlay routing, and mode transitions. This task protects the architectural core from silent regressions as feature work expands.
design: Focus on deterministic state transitions and effect emission. Keep tests small and numerous enough that refactors can rely on them.
acceptance_criteria: Done when core state transitions have direct unit coverage and new reducers can follow the same test pattern.
notes: Failure mode to avoid: only testing through the UI while leaving the state model unguarded.

title: Implement Git integration tests for the planned topology matrix
description: Turn the topology matrix from the plan into executable Git integration tests. This includes clean repos, dirty repos, staged or unstaged mixes, detached HEAD, upstream divergence, conflicts, stashes, and worktrees.
design: Build on the shared fixture harness and make each scenario explicit enough that future backend-routing changes can be validated automatically.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the key Git topology cases from the plan are represented by reproducible integration tests.
notes: Failure mode to avoid: backend behavior changing without anyone noticing because the scenario only existed in the planning document.

title: Add watcher and debounce correctness tests
description: Create tests for watcher invalidation, debounce logic, degraded mode, and recovery. Realtime freshness is one of the differentiators of the product, so it cannot remain a best-effort subsystem.
design: Prefer fake or simulated event sources where possible so tests stay stable and do not depend on platform-specific watcher timing.
acceptance_criteria: Done when watcher semantics and debounce behavior are covered by deterministic tests rather than manual confidence only.
notes: Failure mode to avoid: flaky watcher tests that teams start ignoring.

title: Build a TUI end-to-end harness for keyboard flows with detailed logging
description: Create the end-to-end test harness for critical keyboard workflows such as workspace triage, enter repo mode, stage, commit, and sync. The port is UI-heavy, so reducer and integration tests alone are not enough.
design: Drive the app through realistic key sequences and keep logs detailed enough to debug failures. Cover the highest-value flows first before expanding to advanced cases.
acceptance_criteria: Done when at least the core triage and commit loop can run through an automated end-to-end path with actionable logs on failure.
notes: Failure mode to avoid: an e2e harness so opaque that every failure requires manual reproduction to understand.

title: Add performance harnesses for startup, scan, refresh, and diff loads
description: Implement reproducible performance checks for startup, workspace scan, summary refresh, and repo diff loading. This task protects the low-RAM and fast-start constraints that are central to the product story.
design: Use representative fixture sizes and make the results inspectable in CI or local debug mode. Treat regressions as first-class signals rather than anecdotal complaints.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the project has an explicit way to measure the main latency budgets from the plan.
notes: Failure mode to avoid: performance regressions discovered only after the graph is mostly implemented.

title: Cover failure modes such as missing upstream, auth errors, conflicts, and stale state
description: Add tests and debug paths for the main operational failure modes identified in the plan: missing upstream, transport failure, merge or rebase in progress, conflicts, inaccessible repos, watcher degradation, and stale cache situations.
design: Make these cases visible in both tests and user-facing state transitions. Treat them as normal operational scenarios, not weird edge cases.
acceptance_criteria: Done when the app has explicit coverage for the main ways a Git TUI can lose user trust during everyday use.
notes: Failure mode to avoid: happy-path coverage only.

title: Write operator-facing README, keybinding, config, and troubleshooting docs
description: Create the initial operator-facing documentation set for the product: README framing, keybinding guide, config reference, and troubleshooting notes. This is part of shipping a trustworthy tool, not an afterthought.
design: Document workspace mode versus repo mode clearly and explain the major flows and limitations honestly. Keep docs aligned with the implemented graph rather than the aspirational future-only feature set.
acceptance_criteria: Done when a new user can understand what the tool is for, how to navigate it, how to configure it, and how to debug common problems.
notes: Failure mode to avoid: docs that overpromise parity before the implementation really supports it.

title: Codify the canonical product contract and execution guardrails
description:
Freeze the non-negotiable product contract for super_lazygit_rust at the bead-graph level so future implementation cannot quietly drift into a generic Git TUI, a loose Lazygit clone, or a workspace dashboard bolted onto shallow repo tooling. This bead exists to make the execution graph itself carry the product boundary: outside a repo the app must think like git-dash/git-scope, inside a repo it must feel close to Lazygit, and both scopes must share one coherent Rust architecture built around reducer-driven state, a unified Git facade, watcher invalidation, explicit safety, and early test discipline.

Why this matters: the plan is already broad and detailed, but execution drift is now the main risk. Future agents should not have to rediscover which constraints are load-bearing. The graph must make it obvious that Git correctness outranks convenience, workspace mode is a first-class product surface rather than a thin landing page, repo mode must preserve Lazygit-grade interaction quality, and quality/performance work overlays the implementation instead of being deferred to the end.

What this bead should lock in at the graph level:
- the sharp product rule: git-dash outside the repo, Lazygit inside the repo, Lazygit-like visual language throughout
- architecture guardrails: crate-split workspace, reducer/effect boundaries, no direct UI-to-Git coupling, unified backend policy
- sequencing guardrails: foundation before deep Git work, Git facade before repo/workspace readers, workspace and repo mode both protected from drift, quality tracks attached early enough to validate behavior instead of papering over it later
- operator trust constraints: destructive actions require explicit confirmation, stale-state handling must remain visible, performance must stay acceptable on low-RAM multi-repo setups

Acceptance criteria:
1. The bead graph has one explicit contract/guardrail task under the root epic.
2. The graph structure makes it harder to start deep repo-mode or workspace-mode implementation without first acknowledging the product contract and architectural boundaries.
3. bv --robot-triage still produces a sensible top-of-graph order after this patch, with no cycles introduced.
4. The resulting structure clearly preserves workspace-first scope, Lazygit-grade repo scope, Git correctness, and quality/performance obligations.

Verification note: after updating the graph, run bv --robot-triage and inspect the relevant br show output to confirm the new guardrail bead is present and dependency-safe.
close_reason: Verified guardrail bead present with dependency-safe blocker wiring
